Translated by Helena Kurzová. Europa (Academia). Prague: Academia, 2016. ---. Aeneid: Books 1-6. Edited by Randall T. Ganiban. Focus Vergil Aeneid Commentaries. Newburyport: Focus, 2012. 122 - Shirley Werner Gagliardi gives a history of the translation of the Eclogues into Italian from the Fascist era to the present day, observing that the comparison of translations reveals diverse approaches to poetry and to classical culture. Holzberg's Aeneis (2015) contains an introduction (9-40); Latin text and facing German translation; a note on the Latin text of this edition (675-76); a commentary (677-730); an essay, "Aeneas und Augustus," by Markus Schauer (731-59); and a bibliography (760-66). Perret's Énéide (2013) contains an introduction, Latin text and facing French translation, and notes by Paul Veyne. Trevizam's Geórgicas (2013) includes a Latin text and facing Portuguese translation. Holzberg's edition of the Eclogues and Georgics (2016) offers a Latin text and facing German translation of the poems preceded by an introduction (7-40) and followed by a note on the Latin text of this edition (255-56), a commentary (257-98), an essay, "Pascua und Rura von der Antike bis in die Gegenwart: Zum Fortwirken der Bucolica und Georgica Vergils" (299-327), and a bibliography (328-34). In their German translation and edition Leben auf dem Lande (2013), von Albrecht edited, translated, and wrote commentary for the Bucolica and Schönberger for the Georgica; the edition contains an afterword by von Albrecht. Virgile: Oeuvres complètes (2015) includes notes, a Latin text and facing French translation of the Bucoliques and Énéide by Dion and Heuzé and of the Géorgiques by Dion and Michel, and notes, Latin text, and a French translation of the Appendix Vergiliana. In Zpěvy rolnické (2016), Kurzová translates the Georgics into Czech. Ganiban's introduction to Aen. 1-6 offers a Latin text with commentary in English. Gärtner, Thomas. "Untersuchungen zum pseudo-vergilischen Culex." Acta Antiqua Academiae Scientiarum Hungaricae 50 (2010): 203-47. Kayachev, Boris. Allusion and Allegory: Studies in the Ciris. Beiträge zur Altertumskunde

Biggs argues that "Livy's depiction of Fabius' delay in the mountain pastures
of Campania is enriched by a nexus of imagery, language, and landscape
central to the Latin pastoral genre as shaped by Vergil's Eclogues" (281).

Baraz argues that Calpurnius's conception of nature as obstructive and on
the importance of silence for poetic composition responds to moments of
discord between man and nature in Vergil's Eclogues. Berrens discusses
the influence of Aen. 1.430-36 on Seneca's Epist. 84. Dean's dissertation
studies Perpetua's Passio, Proba's Cento, and Egeria's Itinerarium for what
they can reveal about an educational system that enabled female authorship.
Goodfellow's title quotes Martial 8.55.19. Graverini argues that in describing
Lucius's movement from Greece to Rome at Met. 11.26.1 Apuleius indirectly
involves Africa through a web of clues that connect Hypata, Byrrhena,
and Lucius with Carthage, Dido, and Aeneas. Hinds discusses Ausonius's
acknowledgement, in his secular cento, of Proba's Christian cento. Le
Doze discusses Aen. 12.821-28, 834-40, and other passages. Leroux's
contribution explores the treatment of Iris in Ovid and Seneca based on
their source in Vergil. McCutcheon discusses the imagery of disrupted
pastoral and agrarian landscapes and characters in Vergil's oeuvre and in
Lucan. Power argues that Tacitus's account of Galba's end need not allude
directly to Vergil's Priam. Quartarone considers the word umbra in Vergil,
Ovid, Horace, and the Culex. Rudoni discusses intertextual echoes of Vergil
and Lucretius in Eumolpus's poem. Schottenius Cullhed discusses Jerome,
Epist. 53, where Jerome compares incompetent biblical exegesis to Vergilian
and Homeric centos, with perhaps pointed reference to the work of Proba.

Caldini Montanari observes that Vergil's "cadentia sidera" alludes to Sappho,
fr. 168, and indicates not the end of night but midnight. Coppola points to
details in the Aeneid that may reflect elements of the Alexander legend as
transmitted by Lycophron; these, she argues, should be read in the context
of Augustan propaganda. Fernández Corte considers the intertextual
resonances of Vergil's "sus" at Aen. 8.83 with Lucretius and Horace.
Gagliardi, "Alberi e amore," observes that Vergil uses tree imagery to allude
to Theocritus and Gallus; in "L'Ecl. 10" she remarks on the influence of Bion,
Moschus, and Gallus. Lasagna focuses on the motif of traveling upstream as
it is developed in Aen. 7.36 and 8.86-101 and in Apollonius, Argonautica
2.1260-85. Pellacani argues that the syntactic ambiguity of the phrase "ab
Ioue principium Musae" at Ecl. 3.60 alludes both to Theocritus 17.1 and
to Cicero, Aratus, fr. 1 Soubiran, with its Hesiodic subtext. Wesolowski
discusses tree symbolism in Theocritus, Vergil, and Longus in the context
of the disappointment of personal desires.

Kiss, "Catullo," considers Servius's testimony, echoed by the humanist Guglielmo da Pastrengo (1290-1362), concerning the gender of pumex at Catullus 1.2 and concludes that, even though Servius is not reliable, it is intrinsically credible that pumex may belong to a group of nouns of the third declension in -ex or -is that are attested in both genders. In "Un passo perduto," Kiss discusses Servius's paraphrase, in his commentary on Georg. 2.95, of some polemical remarks made by Catullus concerning "uva Raetica." These words belong to no known poem and may derive from a poem by Catullus now lost but known to Servius. Vergilian Bibliography - 135 Canetta, Isabella.
